opp.name: Domino's S2C + ANA
opp.next_step: 75% confidence 06 JUL - Completed POC planning meeting with Domino's Europe team. Next step is Domino's providing data for Spend POC. Some concerns about executing POC with disparate DPE resources, will work with Jai on this. 22 JUN - POC planning underway, we are waiting for Jai to respond / delegate data gathering for Spend. Should meet up this week in Brisbane. 6th Jun Clarification Q's from Jai (CPO) about the POC metrics and outcomes- what data do we require to run a successful POC etc 29 MAY - Post demo, prospect wants a POC to demonstrate capability and gain internal signoff. Plan for an in principal agreement to award post POC 26 MAY - Demo scheduled for 3rd June 11 <ay - we're in contact and working on a date late May 5th May Spoke to Jai who has asked for a demo to his team as next steps- he is aligning/coaching his team and will revert back to us early May 22nd Apr- Jai Ratongi (CPO) who is ex Thai Union (close to Carl) wants fast eval cycle for ANA- maybe, a mini POC to show stakeholders Wants to show quick value - and extend to CLM this year
opp.ais_why:
• Per the 22 Apr 2026 rep note: 'Jai Ratongi (CPO) who is ex Thai Union (close to Carl) wants fast eval cycle for ANA- maybe, a mini POC to show stakeholders Wants to show quick value - and extend to CLM this year.' This is a direct buyer-side ask: CPO Jai Ratongi is explicitly driving an evaluation for ANA (Autonomous Negotiation Agent) with a mini-POC to prove quick value to internal stakeholders, with CLM as Phase 2.
• On 5 May 2026: 'Spoke to Jai who has asked for a demo to his team as next steps- he is aligning/coaching his team and will revert back to us early May.' On 11 May 2026: 'we're in contact and working on a date late May.' Jai is actively coaching his team for a demo and pushing the eval cycle fast ‚Äî classic AI-champion behaviour, not a passive evaluation. The opp name is literally 'Domino's ANA' and the product scope is iContract; ANA (ANA being the AI/autonomous negotiation product).
• Confidence is High. Domino's is a named-CPO-driven ANA POC with a defined Phase 2 extension to CLM. Caveat: BD_Engagement is Unengaged in SFDC (despite Jai's active championship ‚Äî possibly a data lag), and the formal demo to Jai's team hasn't happened yet. Score 8 reflects buyer-led AI demand with a champion but pending demo execution; would move to 9 with a successful POC.

contacts.title: Group Chief Procurement & Supply Chain Officer

items.quote: Wayne McMahon (CIO) owns the Dynamics ERP and integration path but has not been engaged; the future integration scope was explicitly deferred by the buyer.

items.area: POC success criteria and savings baseline
items.quote: The POC is agreed in principle but explicit, measurable success thresholds and a savings baseline are not yet documented, risking an ambiguous result.
items.source: Task 6 Jun, Jai clarification questions on POC metrics

sources: Avoma demo 3 Jun, Fran (IT procurement team lead)
metrics.narrative: Strong qualitative pain with the ROI case still to be proven on the POC. Jai Rastogi (Group CPO) framed the whole initiative on 3 Jun as funding the company turnaround: share price fell from $161 to $16.68 after hyperinflation with no menu-board increases, and 'the fuel to get fit for growth and generate cash for reinvestment comes from this table' (procurement). Concrete inefficiency was quantified in time: a technology-spend mapping run by Fran's team 'has taken almost 8 weeks' with still no full answer, a basic cheese-spend question returns 'ten different spreadsheets, five of them wrong', and a CEO spend question is 'now 9 months later and we still haven't answered it'. Savings magnitude was illustrated in the demo (dairy consolidation 126 to 300k, an onshore IT-consulting rationalization of 850k, a 600% ROI reference), but these are Zycus examples, not yet Domino's own validated numbers. Quantifying Domino's real savings on their own data is precisely the stated POC goal, so the metric is confirmed as a need and the move is to make the POC output the dollar case.

sources: Task 25 Jun to 2 Jul POC thread
champion.narrative: Jai Rastogi (Group Chief Procurement and Supply Chain Officer) is a strong, active champion. He drove the demo request (5 May), set the agenda and priorities ('Jai has kindly given us pointers on priorities'), led buyer-side participation on the 3 Jun demo, structured the POC, sent clarification questions on POC metrics (6 Jun), asked Luke to prepare aggressive commercial pricing to help the internal sell (10 Jun), and is personally close to Carl Kimball (ex Thai Union). His advocacy is explicit: he told the room 'we're going to do a POC with Zycus' and pre-positioned Zycus with 'you've got a front run before we do anything formal' (29 May). What is still developing is the coalition above and around him: he is delegating POC data work to his EU/AU teams, but the CFO (Lynn Carruthers) and CIO (Wayne McMahon) have not been brought into a call, so the deal is single-threaded at the power level despite broad engagement within procurement. Current engagement is active as of 2 Jul.

competition.narrative: This is a sole-sourced deal whose real opponent is Do Nothing / the status quo of spreadsheets, Jira and manual processes. Jai stated 'I haven't gone out to market to anyone' and gave Zycus 'a front run before we do anything formal' (29 May), and no RFP is running. Jai carries prior deployment experience with Coupa and SAP Ariba (at Thai Union and other organizations) and has evaluated Ivalua, so he benchmarks Zycus against best-of-breed, but none of those vendors is engaged on this opportunity, so they are dormant reference points, not active threats. The wedge is Zycus AI (Merlin) for agentic opportunity discovery and normalization of disparate-ERP data with no common taxonomy, delivered as a fast, modular POC that funds the wider transformation. The genuine risk is not a rival vendor but a weak or slow POC that fails to quantify savings and lets internal momentum revert to status quo or triggers a formal RFP.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is specific, confirmed and owned by the buyer. Jai Rastogi described an enterprise procurement function that is 'very immature' with 'no enablement at all', just launching Dynamics as its ERP and with 'no other source-to-contract capabilities here at all'. Contracts live in Jira with no workflow, compliance 'will be very low', and indirect spend (technology, marketing, travel) is essentially unmanaged with no common taxonomy and inconsistent GL/cost-center coding that his own team admitted causes constant misclassification. Fran owns the concrete example: mapping technology software and licensing spend has taken 8 weeks and is still incomplete. The pain owner is procurement (Jai and his indirect/IT-procurement leads Fran and Sarah), and the finance consequence (funding the turnaround from procurement savings) makes it a board-level pain, not a departmental one.

sources: Task 26 to 27 May, NDA countersigned (Kadie Skehan, ITPP-9599)
paper_process.narrative: Early contracting signals exist but the paper path is not yet formalized. An NDA was countersigned on 27 May by the relevant stakeholder of the Domino's procurement team (reference ITPP-9599), clearing the first legal gate. Jai flagged on 3 Jun that a separate CLM meeting must be set up with Domino's head of legal / chief legal officer, and Zycus confirmed implementation would be delivered in-house (no external systems integrator) via onshore design plus offshore build. There is no signed MSA/SOW, no procurement channel defined, and InfoSec/security has only been touched at a high level (Australian Macquarie data-center hosting was pitched, then Fran raised the European-hosting requirement for a group agreement). The move that creates visibility is to schedule the CLM/legal session and confirm the security and hosting review path.

sources: SF OpportunityContactRole: Lynn Carruthers, Executive Sponsor
economic_buyer.narrative: The economic buyer is identified but not yet engaged. Lynn Carruthers (CFO) is named the Executive Sponsor on the Salesforce contact roles, and Jai has repeatedly tied the decision to finance: he referenced needing 'George' and the CEO to sign off and said 'ultimately we need to fund this, and the best way to fund it is by demonstrating enhanced commercial outcomes' (3 Jun). Jai himself (Group Chief Procurement and Supply Chain Officer) controls the procurement mandate and is driving commercial urgency, asking Luke on 10 Jun to 'get the best commercial pricing as possible, I want to be aggressive to help'. But CFO Lynn Carruthers has appeared on no call and no email thread this sweep, so her buy-in is unverified and the POC ROI is the explicit gate to it. The move is to have Jai bring Lynn into a POC-outcomes and ROI review to pre-wire budget approval.

sources: Task 1 to 2 Jul, Michael Gengenbach POC thread
decision_process.narrative: The process the buyer is running is readable from the call and email sequence: a full-team demo on 3 Jun (Jai plus his IT-procurement leads Fran, Sarah, Kadie, contract/finance lead Annie, KL-based Jasmine, and Supply Chain Manager Vanessa Muller), then a scoped POC on seven-country indirect and technology spend, then an in-principle agreement to award post-POC (29 May Next Step). Jai is delegating the POC data gathering to his EU and AU teams (25 Jun) and Zycus has added a European POC lead, Michael Gengenbach (1 to 2 Jul). A separate CLM track with Domino's chief legal officer is planned but not yet scheduled. What remains unconfirmed is the formal approval chain above Jai: CFO and CEO ('George') sign-off is referenced but the sequence, timing and any procurement/board gate are not documented, so the process is partial pending that mapping.

sources: Avoma demo 3 Jun, Jasmine and Sarah
decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are explicit and buyer-stated. Jai prioritized on 3 Jun the two areas of highest impact: 'the biggest needs we have right now are essentially around spend visibility and analytics to uncover opportunity, and then CLM', with sourcing next, and SRM deliberately deprioritized. The AI capability is the decisive differentiator: 'I was excited when I saw your AI capabilities as a place to accelerate opportunity identification'. The POC itself is the criteria instrument: prove AI-led opportunity discovery and savings on Domino's own messy, disparate-ERP indirect and technology spend across seven countries, with data source-validation demanded by Jasmine and Sarah (the ability to drill from an AI output down to the transaction and its source system). A group requirement of European data hosting was added by the relevant stakeholder are confirmed and documented across the demo dialogue.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. It's genuinely competitive and could go either way; the reads below say where it stands.

blocks.title: What moves it forward

cro_panel.header: DOMINO'S PIZZA ENTERPRISES LIMITED — Formal Evaluation · $450K · closes 28 Aug

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 0: no break-risk was observed in the swept evidence. Coverage is thin, so read this as 'nothing seen yet,' not 'nothing there.'
commentary.win_position: Win 52 is carried by preference strength +1.00 (weight 20); momentum 63 vs stage-expected 52. Offsetting it: differentiation strength -0.40 (weight 20).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 63 (moving forward): commercial topics entering (from call evidence). Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 3 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (exec and champion, commercial, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 45 is anchored on the stage win (52), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 63, and dragged by risk 0. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.

contributions.evidence: customer requested next meeting (from call evidence).
Verdict flags close-date risk this sweep.
Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.

The field is if anything conservative given how AI-forward the buyer is.

deal_movement.summary: Highly active this sweep. Since the 3 Jun full demo, the deal moved into POC scoping: a seven-country indirect/technology spend POC was defined (10 Jun), Jai asked for aggressive pricing (10 Jun), Jai delegated data-gathering to his EU and AU teams (25 Jun), and Zycus added a European POC lead (Michael Gengenbach) with a platform overview held 3 Jul. Amount was raised 200k to 450k on 6 Jun. Engagement is live today.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 22 Apr 2026, 72 days in Formal Evaluation. Close date 28 Aug is 56 days out. POC scope was defined 10 Jun (spend data across seven countries, indirects plus technology) but the seven-country data set is still being delegated by Jai to his EU and AU teams as of 25 Jun to 2 Jul, so the POC has not started. A Zycus EU lead (Michael Gengenbach) was brought on 1 to 2 Jul and a platform overview was held 3 Jul, resetting POC prep. Remaining path: data load, POC execution (2 to 3 weeks minimum), internal cross-functional sign-off, CFO Lynn Carruthers approval, separate CLM legal review, commercials, contracting. 56 days is insufficient for this sequence. Deal is very live: buyer emails 2 Jul, platform session 3 Jul, demo 3 Jun. Forward slip to Q4 2026 probable.
evidence: Deal is live and buyer-engaged: active POC scope email thread 25 Jun to 2 Jul with Jai, platform overview session 3 Jul, full platform demo 3 Jun
POC scope crystallized 10 Jun: cover spend data across seven countries, focus on indirects and technology (not food and paper), prove AI-led opportunity identification

Champion confirmed and active: Jai Rastogi (Group CPO) is driving the eval, coaching the demo agenda, and personally close to Carl Kimball (ex Thai Union); stated Zycus has 'a front run' before anything formal (29 May)

Strong pain confirmed on 3 Jun demo (Jai Rastogi): procurement maturity 'exceptionally low', bodies in spreadsheets, no common taxonomy across disparate ERPs, no CLM workflow (contracts sit in Jira), low compliance and leakage, indirects almost entirely unmanaged

Quantified motivation (Jai, 3 Jun): share price fell from $161 to $16.68 through hyperinflation with no menu increases; transformation must be funded by procurement savings; a CEO spend question is still unanswered 9 months on and a technology-spend mapping has taken 8 weeks with no full answer
Economic Buyer mapped but not engaged: Lynn Carruthers (CFO) is the Executive Sponsor contact role; she has appeared on no call, and POC ROI is the gate to her budget sign-off
New requirement surfaced 3 Jun: European hosting for a group agreement, raised by Fran (IT procurement team lead); Luke committed to find hosting options
Commercial signal: Jai asked Luke on 10 Jun to help secure the most aggressive commercial pricing possible to help the internal sell
Pipeline forecast at this stage and timeline is indefensible; Best Case is the honest category given the active POC and strong champion, but the 28 Aug date will slip to Q4 2026
north_star_verdict.headline: Healthy, buyer-driven POC-stage deal with a strong CPO champion and clear pain, but the 28 Aug close is not credible with POC data still being gathered, the CFO not yet engaged, and a separate legal CLM review and contracting still ahead.

flags: Multi-thread to power: engagement is broad within procurement but CFO Lynn Carruthers (budget) and CIO Wayne McMahon (integration) are still untouched; route the POC ROI to Lynn to fund the award.
De-risk the POC: agree written success criteria and a savings baseline with Jai up front so the seven-country result is unambiguous and directly funds the business case.
Clear the European-hosting gate early: Fran flagged it as a standard requirement for group agreements, so confirm the EU hosting option before it blocks a group contract.
best_practice_check.summary: Sole-sourced, champion-led deal whose whole value hinges on the POC. Highest-leverage levers now: multi-thread up to power, make the POC result unambiguous, and clear the group hosting gate early.

customer_preference.evidence: Jai: 'we're going to do a POC with Zycus', 'you've got a front run before we do anything formal', and asked Luke to prepare aggressive pricing to sell internally.

competitive_position.summary: No active vendor competition. The primary competitor is Do Nothing (status quo: spreadsheets, Jira, manual processes). Jai Rastogi (CPO) has prior experience deploying Coupa and SAP Ariba at Thai Union and other orgs and evaluated Ivalua, but stated 'I haven't gone out to market to anyone' and 'you've got a front run' before any formal RFP (29 May 2026 call). Zycus is sole-sourced and the deal is ours to lose. The strongest current threat is reversion to Do Nothing if POC results are weak or data quality prevents clear savings quantification, which would stall internal momentum and could trigger a formal RFP to Coupa or Ariba.

competitors.name: Do Nothing (status quo)
competitors.quote: Procurement maturity is exceptionally low. Bodies in spreadsheets. No source-to-contract technology. No CLM system, poor contract compliance, significant leakage. Indirect spend completely unmanaged.
competitors.how_we_win: Deliver strong POC ROI proof on their own disparate-ERP indirect and technology spend using Merlin AI to normalize data and surface savings, show quick wins on spend insights and CLM, secure CFO sponsorship, and move before momentum reverts to status quo.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: I've worked with everyone from Ivalua to Coupa to Ariba. I've deployed Coupa and Ariba at Thai Union and other organizations.
competitors.how_we_win: Jai knows Coupa from prior deployments but has not engaged them for this eval; keep the front run by proving a faster AI POC-to-value cycle before any formal RFP opens.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: I've worked with everyone from Ivalua to Coupa to Ariba. I've deployed Coupa and Ariba at Thai Union and other organizations.
competitors.how_we_win: Jai knows Ariba from prior deployments but has not engaged them for this eval; win by demonstrating superior AI and faster POC-to-value before a formal RFP.

competitors.name: Ivalua
competitors.quote: I've worked with everyone from Ivalua to Coupa to Ariba.
competitors.how_we_win: Jai evaluated Ivalua in prior roles but has not engaged them here; keep the front run by proving quick, AI-led savings on their own data.

items.deliverable: Send Fran the available European data-hosting options for Zycus
items.grounding_quote: We have a number of options in Europe. I don't know what they are off the top of my head, but I can find out some information for you there.

items.deliverable: Provide seven-country indirect and technology spend data to run the POC
items.grounding_quote: I am assigning to one of my team in EU and will be discussing with them next week. In the interim you will start to receive details from my AU team.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI is the centre of gravity of this deal, not an add-on. The opportunity is literally named for ANA (Autonomous Negotiation Agent), Merlin runs through every demoed module, and Jai said the AI capability is what excited him and what the POC must prove. Positioning is landing well.

items.quote: I was excited when I saw your AI capabilities as a place to accelerate opportunity identification.
items.criterion: AI-led opportunity discovery on messy, disparate-ERP indirect spend

items.quote: Will the output provide the source from where the numbers are derived... a point of reference for us.
items.criterion: Spend visibility and analytics with drill-down to transaction source

items.quote: CLM from a workflow and enablement from an AI perspective and just having a central repository is a big gap and a need for us.
items.criterion: CLM workflow and central contract repository (replacing Jira)

items.quote: Is there options for European hosting? The standard position has historically been, when doing group agreements, European hosting.
items.criterion: European data hosting for a group agreement

items.quote: The best way to fund it is by demonstrating that we can drive enhanced commercial outcomes from procurement.
items.criterion: Quantified, board-grade savings ROI to fund the transformation

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong alignment: the buyer's stated priorities (spend visibility and analytics, then CLM, then sourcing, all AI-led) map directly to Zycus modules and the ANA/Merlin story. The main exposure is a group European-hosting requirement and the fact that all value is still to be proven on the POC.

hard.ais_why:
• Per the 22 Apr 2026 rep note: 'Jai Ratongi (CPO) who is ex Thai Union (close to Carl) wants fast eval cycle for ANA- maybe, a mini POC to show stakeholders Wants to show quick value - and extend to CLM this year.' This is a direct buyer-side ask: CPO Jai Ratongi is explicitly driving an evaluation for ANA (Autonomous Negotiation Agent) with a mini-POC to prove quick value to internal stakeholders, with CLM as Phase 2.
• On 5 May 2026: 'Spoke to Jai who has asked for a demo to his team as next steps- he is aligning/coaching his team and will revert back to us early May.' On 11 May 2026: 'we're in contact and working on a date late May.' Jai is actively coaching his team for a demo and pushing the eval cycle fast ‚Äî classic AI-champion behaviour, not a passive evaluation. The opp name is literally 'Domino's ANA' and the product scope is iContract; ANA (ANA being the AI/autonomous negotiation product).
• Confidence is High. Domino's is a named-CPO-driven ANA POC with a defined Phase 2 extension to CLM. Caveat: BD_Engagement is Unengaged in SFDC (despite Jai's active championship ‚Äî possibly a data lag), and the formal demo to Jai's team hasn't happened yet. Score 8 reflects buyer-led AI demand with a champion but pending demo execution; would move to 9 with a successful POC.
